learn.title: Demand Response Power Balancing
learn.intro: Demand response lets big energy users, like factories and hospitals, shift or cut power use when the grid needs it. This smart flexibility keeps supply and demand matched, reduces outages, and lets renewable sources like wind and solar integrate more smoothly into the power system.
learn.c1_text: Think of a thermostat that turns down heating when the room is too warm. Large consumers lower their power draw during peak hours, saving the grid from overload. A single 500 kW industrial plant can cut 20 % of its load in 30 minutes, easing grid stress by 100 MW.
learn.c2_text: Grid operators send price signals or alerts. When electricity prices spike, businesses automatically reduce load or shift it to off‑peak. This rapid response can happen in seconds, preventing brownouts and allowing more renewable energy to stay on the line.
learn.c3_text: India’s industrial sector, producing 1.2 trillion kWh annually, could supply up to 30 GW of DR capacity. The government’s DR pilot in Maharashtra cut peak demand by 10 % during a 3‑hour window, saving $2 million in avoided costs.
learn.stat2: DR pilots saved $2 million in Maharashtra (2023)
learn.stat3: Potential DR capacity: 30 GW in India

learn.title: Tidal Stream Power
learn.intro: Tidal stream turbines capture the steady, predictable flow of ocean currents, offering a reliable, carbon‑free energy source. Unlike waves or tides, these currents flow continuously, enabling high capacity factors and steady baseload power that complements solar and wind.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a river in the sea. A 3 MW tidal turbine can generate 3 MW continuously, like a 1,200‑kW home running 24/7, producing 26 GWh annually—more than a typical household’s yearly consumption.
learn.c2_text: Tidal flows are known months in advance, giving grid operators exact forecasts. A 10 MW plant can deliver 70 % capacity factor, comparable to wind farms, but with no weather variability.
learn.c3_text: India’s 7,500 km coastline hosts currents like the Gulf of Kutch, capable of 5 GW of tidal potential. Pilot projects in Tamil Nadu are testing 1 MW turbines, aiming for commercial deployment by 2030.
learn.stat2: Potential tidal capacity: 5 GW in India
learn.stat4: Pilot project: 1 MW turbine in Tamil Nadu

learn.title: Solid‑State Batteries
learn.intro: Solid‑state batteries replace liquid electrolytes with solid ones, boosting safety, energy density, and lifespan. This tech can power electric vehicles for 600 km on a single charge and store renewable energy more efficiently, accelerating the shift to clean transport and grids.
learn.c1_text: Instead of flammable liquid, solid batteries use ceramic or glass electrolytes, reducing fire risk. A 100 kWh solid‑state pack can weigh 30 % less than a lithium‑ion pack, improving vehicle range by 25 %.
learn.c2_text: Solid‑state cells pack 200 Wh/kg compared to 150 Wh/kg for conventional batteries, meaning more power in the same space. This allows cars to travel 600 km on a single charge, aligning with India’s 2025 EV targets.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of Heavy Industries aims for 30 % EV adoption by 2030. Solid‑state batteries could reduce vehicle cost by 15 % and increase range, helping meet the 10 GW EV charging infrastructure goal.
learn.stat2: Vehicle range: 600 km on single charge
learn.stat4: India: 10 GW EV charging infrastructure goal (2030)

learn.title: Second‑Life EV Batteries
learn.intro: Used EV batteries still retain 70–80 % capacity and can be repurposed for grid storage, reducing waste and cost. They smooth renewable supply, provide frequency regulation, and extend the life of both batteries and the grid infrastructure.
learn.c1_text: A used EV pack with 70 % capacity can store 70 kWh, enough to power 200 homes for a day. Recycling this material saves 30 % of the energy needed to produce a new battery.
learn.c2_text: Second‑life batteries charge during solar peaks and discharge to the grid during demand spikes, acting like a 5 MW/10 MWh battery farm for a city district, stabilizing supply without new construction.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Electric Mobility Mission Programme plans to repurpose 500 kWh of second‑life batteries by 2028, reducing e‑waste and supporting the 100 GW solar goal by providing backup during night hours.
learn.stat1: Used EV battery capacity: 70 % of original
learn.stat2: Power: 5 MW / 10 MWh battery farm example

learn.title: Solar Inverter Types
learn.intro: Inverters convert the DC electricity from solar panels into AC usable by homes and the grid. Understanding string, micro, and hybrid inverters helps homeowners choose the right system for efficiency, cost, and grid interaction.
learn.c1_text: A single inverter powers an entire string of panels. If one panel dips, the whole string’s output drops, much like a row of candles: if one goes out, the others are dimmer.
learn.c2_text: Each panel has its own inverter, so a shaded panel doesn’t affect the rest. This setup can boost energy harvest by 10–15 % in partially shaded homes, ideal for urban rooftops.
learn.c3_text: Hybrid models combine solar, battery, and grid power, letting Indian households store excess solar and use it at night, supporting the 100 GW solar target by reducing grid draw during peak demand.
learn.stat3: Hybrid inverter market share: 25 % of new installations (2023)

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage
learn.intro: Pumped hydro uses two reservoirs at different elevations to store energy as potential gravity. It’s the world’s largest and most mature battery, providing grid stability, frequency regulation, and long‑term storage for intermittent renewables.
learn.c1_text: Water pumped uphill during low demand uses 10 kWh of electricity per cubic meter. When demand rises, the water flows back down, generating 10 kWh, making it a 100 % round‑trip efficiency system.
learn.c2_text: India’s Tamil Nadu pumped hydro plant stores 400 MW for 12 hours, supplying 4,800 MWh, enough to power 4 million homes for a day, stabilizing the grid during solar dips.
learn.c3_text: India has 35 GW of pumped hydro potential, 10 % of its total hydro capacity, offering a scalable solution to store the 100 GW of planned solar and wind energy.
learn.stat3: Tamil Nadu plant: 400 MW / 12 h storage
